data.title: Property manager in Hong Kong fire case defends ‘admin role’, watchdog disagrees
data.description:
Hong Kong’s property management watchdog has vowed to rigorously follow up on any suspected breaches over the Wang Fuk Court fire that has claimed 159 lives, even as the estate manager described its role as confined to providing administrative support and managing day-to-day security services.
In reply to the Post’s queries, the Property Management Services Authority said on Wednesday that under its Code of Conduct, licensed managers were required to follow up in a timely manner with the engaged fire service installation contractor and to notify the director of fire services at the “soonest possible [time] or in advance” if safety installations had been shut down for repair.
Earlier in the day, Commissioner of Police Joe Chow Yat-ming revealed that six more people had been arrested in connection with the Tai Po inferno, taking the total to 21. Contractors responsible for the fire alarm system at the eight-block estate, which had been undergoing extensive renovations, were the latest to be detained.
Chow said the six were suspected to have falsely declared to the Fire Services Department that they would not deactivate the fire alarm system during renovation work.
The department earlier said the alarm system did not sound when the blaze broke out on Wednesday last week and swept through seven residential blocks.
On Monday, the regulator said Wang Fuk Court’s property management company, ISS EastPoint, was no different from other licensed firms. It said such companies must remind and assist owners in fulfilling their responsibilities, including the proper management and maintenance of fire service installations in common areas of the property, to prevent casualties or loss arising from fire.

The regulator on Wednesday warned that a licensed property management company found guilty of “misconduct or neglect in a professional respect” was committing a disciplinary offence and, upon conviction, would be subject to fines, suspension or revocation of licences.
The Post reported on Monday that ISS EastPoint, a subsidiary of the Danish facilities behemoth ISS, was Wang Fuk Court’s property management company and had remained silent until contacted. On Tuesday, the firm insisted it held no decision-making power over the tendering process at the estate.
The company is among key parties with a role in the renovation work, along with main contractor Prestige Construction and Engineering Co Ltd, project consultant Will Power Architects Co Ltd, the estate owners’ corporation and subcontractors.
The concept of “occupier’s liability”, according to the Occupiers Liability Ordinance, imposes a duty on the party in control of the premises to ensure the safety of visitors.
A property manager can be regarded as an occupier where it has control over the daily management of a building, according to the website of law firm Herbert Smith Freehills Kramer.
Tony Tse Wai-chuen, lawmaker for the architectural, surveying, planning and landscape sector, said that a property manager’s duty went beyond administrative tasks to the “constant” operations of a building. Among its roles was inspecting whether relevant systems operated properly and reporting to owners.
“Fire safety equipment should be regularly inspected or tested,” Tse said. “If problems are discovered, they should identify what the problem is so it can be fixed quickly.”
He added that while management companies were generally expected to provide an analysis of tenders for owners to make decisions, they should seek external help for complex issues.
“If the matter is of a complex nature and the property management company itself lacks the professional knowledge, it might appoint experts in this field to give advice,” he said. “It depends on the scope of the work involved.”

Records show, however, the management office, or ISS, was well aware of the estate’s deteriorating fire safety infrastructure long before the blaze, and tabled tenders for repair work.
Minutes from a meeting on November 7, less than three weeks before the fire, reveal that the estate had a leaking fire tank gate valve on the roof of a block, Wang Chi House, that required urgent repair, with members of the estate management committee of the owners’ corporation suggesting asking Prestige for a quote.
In late September, the committee approved a HK$144,170 contract to repair equipment that specifically included “fire alarm bells” and “manual break-glass units” across the estate, the very systems that failed to sound when smoke engulfed the towers.
Minutes from May show the committee approved repairs for a leaking fire inlet pipe at Wang Yan House, awarding the job to Victory Fire Engineering, which offered the lowest bid at HK$8,500 among three competitors.
In July, the committee approved HK$97,200 from the renovation contingency fund to replace “ageing and rusted” fire tank pipes and accessories across four towers, Wang Kin, Wang Tai, Wang Shing and Wang Chi, after defects were exposed during waterproofing works.
The Hong Kong Association of Property Management Companies on Wednesday said that there were “protocols and responsibilities in relation to fire safety and project management”.
“When a central fire alarm is defective or under repair, our top priority is resident safety,” the association said, adding that increased human patrols, pushing for fast repairs and sending alerts to residents and ensuring staff were around to help in emergencies were important duties of a property manager.
As professional managers, the association said it was important to communicate when selecting the critical contracts, helping the owners’ corporation make fully informed financial decisions.
“We ensure that contractors meet the necessary statutory licences and standards before providing quotations,” it added.
Earlier the Post revealed a series of safety breaches by main contractor Prestige.

data.title: Digging through Hong Kong’s disaster site left them wanting to cry
data.description:
Hazy air laced with a pungent odour, dark rooms filled with debris, bodies that had to be carefully extracted by hand. The discovery of a dead child.
The head of the police team responsible for the search of the Hong Kong residential complex destroyed in an inferno gave an account on Wednesday of the harrowing conditions they faced inside the seven towers as work came to an end.
The task of checking the more than 1,000 flats at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po was both physically and emotionally challenging, said Superintendent Cheng Ka-chun, the officer in charge of the Disaster Victim Identification Unit.
“Our colleagues, in a bid to keep the bodies more intact for their return to their families, chose to dig for the bodies using their hands, touching sand, mud and polluted water,” he said, explaining why his team had decided to recover the corpses by hand instead of using equipment.
“Their only goal was to preserve the integrity of the bodies.”

As of Wednesday, a week after the inferno began at Wang Cheong House, police have tallied 159 deaths, with the victims ranging in age from one to 97. Commissioner of Police Joe Chow Yat-ming has said the toll may rise further.
Seventy bodies were found in Wang Cheong House. Another 82 were discovered in Wang Tai House, three in Wang Sun House, two in Wang Tao House and one in Wang Shing House.




The remaining deceased victim was believed to have escaped from one of the buildings, but no one had identified the body yet, according to police.
Firefighters had provided information about the location of many bodies found during their search for survivors, which ended on Friday.
But Cheng’s 600 officers discovered 31 more bodies as they carefully checked the flats in all seven buildings.
They were only recently able to enter flats that were previously inaccessible due to structural damage.
The air inside the buildings was filled with particulates and reeked. There was little natural light during the daytime, and searchers had to rely on headlamps and handheld torches to guide their movements.
Mainland Chinese authorities also supplied mobile lighting equipment, but the team decided the most efficient way to complete the search was maximising efforts during the daytime.
“On one of the floors we searched, we found multiple bodies, including both young and old,” Cheng said. “They could have been a family.”

Wang Fuk Court’s eight 31-storey towers are among the tallest in the district, and searchers had to constantly walk up flights of stairs dressed in their full-body protective garb while carrying equipment.
At the same time, as they steadily advanced through the buildings, methodically combing through each floor, they were confronted with grim scenes that were emotionally draining.
“Some of my team members wanted to cry at the site, but they tried their best to suppress their feelings to avoid hampering work progress,” Cheng said.
One of his team members lost a relative while the search operation was being carried out, but the officer decided to return to work despite his grief.
“His passion has touched me a lot, and I have been a commander for six years,” Cheng said. “I take my hat off to the professionalism and attitude of my teammates.”

Police chief Chow said searchers had also encountered remains that would need DNA testing to determine whether they were human or animal.
Time would also be needed to check whether any bodies were buried beneath collapsed scaffolding and officers would work with housing authorities to remove the bamboo for investigation, Chow said.
Choking back tears, Chief Superintendent Karen Tsang Shuk-yin, who is the officer in charge of the Casualty Inquiry Unit handling calls reporting missing loved ones, earlier said on Monday that the bodies of some victims might never be found.
“Since some of the bodies have already turned to ash, we cannot exclude the possibility that we cannot bring all missing people out,” she said.

data.title: Trump’s second term ‘harmful’ for Quad nations: Australia, Japan, India, poll finds
data.description:
A majority of Australians, Japanese and Indians believe US President Donald Trump’s second term has been bad for their countries, and more Australians see the US as harmful than helpful in Asia, a survey of people in the four countries reported on Thursday.
The United States Studies Centre at the University of Sydney polled 1,000 people in each of the four Quad nations: Australia, Japan, India, and the US. It found the region is shaken by disruptive US developments but still backs coordinated security responses to security challenges.
The poll, taken in August, found 56 per cent of Australians, 54 per cent of Indians and 59 per cent of Japanese think Trump’s presidency has been bad for their countries.
Only 42 per cent of Australians said the US alliance made their country more secure, a 14-point decrease since 2024 and the first time support has dropped below a majority since the centre’s polling began in 2022.
The number of Japanese respondents who said the US alliance made them more secure fell by 5 percentage points from a year ago to only 47 per cent.
“All four countries are deeply concerned about domestic political violence and misinformation in the United States, and Australians and Indians are more likely to see the United States as harmful rather than helpful in Asia,” the report said.

Support for maintaining alliances with the United States continued nonetheless, it found.
Negative perceptions of China persisted from previous surveys, with more Australian (48 per cent), American (40 per cent), Japanese (58 per cent), and Indian (46 per cent) respondents viewing China as harmful rather than helpful in Asia.
Japanese respondents were the most likely to say their country’s handling of China is too weak (59 per cent), the survey found.
On Aukus, although the survey found support across the four countries for the nuclear-powered submarine partnership between Australia, Britain and the US, it noted only a quarter (27 per cent) of Australians think their government has properly explained why Australia needs the submarines.

data.title: Beijing slams ‘external hostile forces’ for exploiting deadly fire in Hong Kong
data.description:
Beijing’s national security arm in Hong Kong on Wednesday condemned “external hostile forces” for exploiting the deadly Tai Po fire that killed at least 159 people, as police arrested a man after he allegedly made derogatory online comments about the victims.
In a strongly worded post, a spokesman for the Office for Safeguarding National Security condemned “a small clique of external hostile forces” for “stirring up trouble and taking advantage of the chaos” in the aftermath of the fire.
The spokesman also accused such forces of disrupting and undermining disaster relief and recovery efforts, as they replicated the tactics employed during the anti-government protests in 2019 under the guise of “advocating for the people”.
“Their intentions are sinister, their conduct despicable, and their actions provoke universal indignation,” he said, adding that they showed cold-blooded indifference and inhumanity when lives were at stake.
The inferno that raged through seven of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks in Tai Po for 43 hours last week has killed 159 people and injured 79 others.
The post said that “the external, anti-China and destabilising forces exploiting the disaster to sow disorder should not be tolerated”.
They will “bear the consequences for adding fuel to the fire” and that “nothing would escape justice”.
“This constitutes a blatant challenge to the fundamental principles of humanity and social morality, inflicting secondary trauma upon the victims. Where is justice?” he said, decrying their actions as “a desecration of Hong Kong’s Lion Rock spirit of resilience in adversity”.
“All acts and words that disrupt Hong Kong will be documented and pursued for life; all conspiracies to destabilise Hong Kong will be exposed and utterly defeated,” he declared in the roughly 1,000-word post.
Four people have been arrested on suspicion of seditious intent under the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance.

Among them was a 26-year-old man arrested by national security police on Wednesday after his online channel “Kowloon King” allegedly made derogatory comments about the victims of the fire, including claims that they were “harbouring grave sins” and “got their retribution” in the tragedy.
A source said the YouTuber, identified as “Kenny Chan”, was a member of “Is Time To Win”, a controversial group known for filming and posting attention-seeking behaviour in public.
On Tuesday, Hong Kong Association for Democracy and People’s Livelihood chairman Bruce Liu Sing-lee, a former district councillor, was summoned for questioning.
Seven other organisers of a civil society press conference on follow-up policies relating to the aftermath of the Tai Po fire were also summoned.
On Saturday, national security police arrested a person called Miles Kwan on suspicion of trying to incite sedition after he and a group of unidentified others set up the Tai Po Wang Fuk Court Fire Concern Group and launched a petition calling on the government to address four demands in the aftermath of the inferno.
The petition called for providing support for affected residents, examining the system for supervising construction work, setting up an independent commission of inquiry and pursuing accountability while holding government officials responsible.
During the 2019 anti-government protests, demonstrators rallied behind a list of demands and chanted the slogan “five demands, not one less”.
Separately, police on Tuesday arrested a 32-year-old cook who allegedly defrauded four people of HK$25,500 (US$3,210) by posing as a victim of the Tai Po fire.
Senior Superintendent Frances Lee King-hei said the man was arrested after the police received reports from four people who suspected they had been scammed between November 29 and December 1.
The man, who is not a Tai Po resident, lied that he lost his family and home during the fire and asked kind-hearted people to give him money.

data.title: Is China’s rapid progress in anti-submarine drones adding to concerns of Japan, US?
data.description:
When a full-scale model of the world’s first independent submarine hunter drone was unveiled at the Dubai Airshow last month, it offered a glimpse of the latest in a series of advances in China’s development of a modern integrated anti-submarine system.
The Wing Loong-X export variant unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) has anti-submarine capabilities that are reportedly superior in some respects to the United States Navy’s Boeing P-8 Poseidon and other manned aircraft.
The full-scale model drone’s appearance in Dubai followed official confirmation that the Chinese navy’s latest ship-based anti-submarine helicopter, the Z-20F, was on board the aircraft carrier Fujian, commissioned earlier in November.
Meanwhile, at the Victory Day parade in September, China displayed its new generation of large anti-submarine and maritime surveillance aircraft, the Y-9Q, together with two unmanned underwater vehicle (UUV) types, the HSU100 and AJX002.
All represent China’s effort to develop an anti-submarine warfare (ASW) system that can counter the challenges posed by rivals operating in the region, including Japan and its strategic partner the US.
The Japan Maritime Self-Defence Force and the US Navy operate large and powerful submarine fleets that have long held critical choke points for the PLA Navy along the first island chain.


Beijing-Tokyo tensions have intensified since November, when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said that an attack on Taiwan by the People’s Liberation Army could constitute a “survival-threatening situation” – one that could allow Tokyo to engage in military action.
It was the most explicit statement by a sitting Japanese prime minister on how Tokyo might respond to a Taiwan contingency and marked a departure from the country’s long-held strategic ambiguity over the issue – a position the US has also maintained.
Takaichi’s remarks crossed a red line on the core issue of Taiwan, which Beijing sees as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary.
Most countries, including the US and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Amid the tensions, Japanese Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi announced that preparations were “progressing steadily” for the deployment of an air-to-surface missile system on Japan’s westernmost island Yonaguni, only 110km (68 miles) from Taiwan.
Koizumi said Tokyo was considering exporting the medium-range system to Manila, which has also seen heightened tensions with Beijing in the South China Sea in recent years.
Rising geopolitical tensions have accelerated the arms race in the region, driving the rapid modernisation of the PLA Navy’s anti-submarine warfare capabilities.




The new variant of the long-range, medium-altitude, heavy-load Wing Loong X has integrated maritime surveillance sensors to detect and track submarines.
Like crewed ASW aircraft, it can deploy sonar buoys, conduct acoustic analysis, coordinate with other airborne or naval platforms in real time and launch advanced anti-submarine torpedoes.
The drone’s key advantage lies in its exceptional endurance, giving it an extended loitering capability over critical maritime zones. It can remain airborne for up to 40 hours – far surpassing the P-8 Poseidon’s maximum flight time of about 10 hours.
The domestically developed Z-20F, a medium-lift helicopter with a range of 1,000km and an endurance of five hours, has the latest sensors and armaments, as well as advanced datalinks, benchmarked with Sikorsky MH-60 Seahawks.
The Z-20F is in the process of replacing the outdated Ka-28 and Z-9C helicopters on board China’s destroyers, especially the new Type 052D and Type 055.
These new destroyers – and some new frigates like the 054A – also have greatly improved anti-submarine capabilities, such as combined active/passive towed array sonars and variable depth sonars.
The combined transition of ships and helicopters, along with the continued rapid expansion of the fleet, is significantly improving the Chinese navy’s ASW reach and capabilities.


The Y-9Q reconnaissance aircraft, with updated AESA radars, magnetic anomaly detector and wingtip electronic sensor, is comparable to the US Lockheed P-3 Orion.
It has a much larger detection area, better locating accuracy and stronger multi-target processing than the first-generation Y-8Q but remains based on the turboprop-powered Y-9 platform. This effectively makes it lag behind the jet-powered P-8 in speed and performance.
There has been speculation that China’s next generation of ASW aircraft could be developed from its domestically produced C919 passenger jet.
The PLA’s extra-large UUVs that were unveiled at the military parade – one designed for reconnaissance and strike operations and the other for minelaying – are believed to have important roles in ASW.
These large drones can be deployed for extended periods, functioning as mobile nodes within China’s expanding “Underwater Great Wall” – a surveillance system comprising fixed seabed-mounted fibre-optic hydrophone arrays, distributed sensor nodes and swarms of smaller autonomous UUVs and surface drones operating in near-seas regions.
The system continuously collects oceanic acoustic data, including signature profiles of adversary submarines.
When activated, the extra-large UUVs can transition from surveillance to combat roles, acting as “loyal wingmen” alongside crewed submarines, surface warships and ASW aircraft to directly engage and neutralise enemy submarines.

data.title: Back to the drawing board: EU revives faltering China de-risking plans
data.description:
As it fights to survive in the new era of economic warfare, the European Union wants to turbo-boost efforts to de-risk its ties with China, using two new proposals.
Launched on Wednesday, the blueprints will combine to upgrade the bloc’s economic arsenal and unpick its heavy reliance on China for critical minerals, including rare earths.
The first, an economic security communication, will provide the EU with an “operating system” on which to manage its many trade, competition and economic weapons – that could be used to punch back against coercion, bullying or the weaponisation of dependencies.
Building on the bloc’s first economic security strategy launched 2½ years ago, it will seek to put long-running risk assessments, conducted by the European Commission and the 27 member states in sectors deemed critical, into action.
A new EU intelligence centre will track economic threats in real time – monitoring if China or others were trying to sabotage Europe’s efforts to reduce dependencies, and maintaining a blacklist of risky companies that should be blocked from EU contracts and funding.
The aim, a senior EU official said, was to forge a “culture of preparedness”, something the bloc has lacked throughout a bruising year.
China’s rare earth controls caught Brussels flat-footed and the EU’s hesitation over US tariff pressure showed how little political alignment there was behind the economic security agenda.
With tools such as an anti-coercion instrument, foreign subsidies regulation, international procurement instrument, and sanctioning measures developed in recent years, the commission does not want immediately to develop new weapons.
Instead, it will use the existing tools more aggressively and with overall resilience objectives in mind.
Anti-dumping and subsidy probes – commonly used against China – will now be opened with economic security in view. The commission wants to assess not only evidence of unfair trade, but also the risk that Chinese practices could wipe out a critical European sector altogether, senior sources said.
The commission says that while Europe will remain open to foreign investors, it will impose tighter conditions on high risk investments to prevent new dependencies, protect sensitive tech and data, and safeguard critical supply chains.


The commission also pledged to assess by the third quarter of next year whether a new trade instrument was needed for dealing with “negative global market developments, such as overcapacity”.
“To stay resilient in a shifting geopolitical and geoeconomic landscape, we must use our tools more strategically and assertively, while developing new ones to reinforce our economic security,” said trade chief Maros Sefcovic.
The second proposal is an action plan to decouple from China’s dominance of minerals modelled on the bloc’s move away from Russian energy following the invasion of Ukraine in 2022, which was known as RePower EU.
Through ReSource EU, which will speed up work under the 2023 Critical Raw Materials Act, the commission wants to restrict exports of scrap rare earth permanent magnets by mid-next year, a move it says could meet 20 per cent of EU demand.
It estimates that EU industry uses 20,000 tonnes of permanent magnets each year, around 18,000 tonnes of which come from China. The overall value of those magnets is relatively small, but the dependence is acute.
The commission estimates that with this initiative, it can reduce dependency on China for rare earth extraction to 42 per cent by 2030, or 60 per cent for processing and 80 per cent for permanent magnets. It hopes to completely eliminate its reliance on Chinese germanium by the same year, and reduce gallium dependence from 71 per cent to 17 per cent, a fact sheet said.
Big European companies that rely on Chinese minerals will have to prove they are reducing that dependence – and if they do not act voluntarily, Brussels can force them to diversify.
“Obviously, when China announces export restriction measures like they have in the past, it creates turmoil in our supply chains. We’re not talking about billions of euros, but millions of euros that are extremely important for the economy,” a second senior official said.
The bloc will pump €3 billion (US$3.5 billion) into projects for critical minerals such as rare earth magnets, lithium and cobalt, and encourage stockpiling and joint purchasing through a new critical raw materials centre. Four projects, including a molybdenum mine in Greenland seen as crucial to Europe’s efforts to rearm and a Czech lithium facility that will provide battery materials, will be fast-tracked.
In a move that may draw some ire from businesses, it will also introduce non-price criteria to ensure costlier European-produced minerals are prioritised in key sectors such as defence, over cheaper Chinese alternatives.
“If we want to diversify, at least in the short term, we will need to pay a bit more than what the Chinese can offer on the market,” said the second official.


In addition, Chinese-linked entities in Europe and China will be blocked from receiving any funding from the EU’s Horizon programme for projects related to critical raw materials, for the years 2026 and 2027.
Next week, meanwhile, the EU is expected to unveil plans to rival China’s own industrial plans with “made in Europe” thresholds of up to 70 per cent local requirements for products like electric vehicles and batteries, the Financial Times reported.
Wednesday’s twin proposals come after a brutal year for Europe following the return of US President Donald Trump. It has been pummelled by both Washington and Beijing, with its deep vulnerabilities to superpower moves exposed once again.
As the bloc faced up to the expected end to the American security blanket, its efforts to rearm were hit by Beijing slapping export licensing requirements on the rare earth elements and magnets that are vital to many areas of defence production.
As it scrambled to keep the US engaged in Ukraine’s defence, meanwhile, it was forced to accept a lopsided trade deal that exposed its lack of autonomy, despite years of striving for it.
Despite the geoeconomic reality, economic security efforts have been abortive amid squabbling between Brussels and the bloc’s 27 member states. An economic security strategy was launched in June 2023 but several senior figures recently admitted the commission still did not have a proper definition.
None of the tools proposed in that strategy – an upgrade to the EU’s inbound investment screening regime, new outbound investment screening measures and unified export controls across the 27 members – have been adopted.
Progress on the de-risking agenda launched to great fanfare by commission chief Ursula von der Leyen in March 2023 has been lacklustre, with businesses reluctant to wind down ties with China and member governments unsure of handing more powers to Brussels on what would ordinarily be considered matters of national sovereignty.

data.title: Shanghai launches clampdown on property market doom-mongering
data.description:
Shanghai has launched a crackdown on online content that talks down the property market, as the sector remains mired in a prolonged downturn and two leading data providers pull back on releasing updated sales figures for China’s top developers.
The Shanghai branch of the Cyberspace Administration of China said it had instructed platforms including RedNote and Bilibili – often dubbed China’s answer to Instagram and YouTube – to remove content deemed to be spreading misleading or alarmist information about the real estate market.
The “special campaign” – which began in mid-November – had already led to the removal of 40,000 posts and caused 70,000 accounts and 1,200 live-streaming rooms to be shut down, the body announced via its official WeChat account on Tuesday.
Some of the accounts were targeted for using phrases that could spread panic among users, such as “the market is about to crash”, “future sales will be impossible” or “the market will collapse”, according to the notice.
Others were accused of exaggerating or misinterpreting housing policies, shaping false market expectations or distorting information about Shanghai’s housing market, it added.
The clampdown comes as concerns continue to swirl over China’s real estate market, with investment in the sector falling, sales remaining sluggish, and analysts warning of a possible wave of foreclosures that could put further downward pressure on home prices.
Meanwhile, two of the country’s top private real estate data providers – China Real Estate Information Corp and China Index Academy – have added to the unease by not publishing their usual monthly reports on the top 100 developers’ sales for November.
If expectations management fails, it could trigger broader risks such as widespread mortgage defaults and a surge in foreclosures
Peng Peng, Guangdong Society of Reform
The firms offered no explanation, but Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter, that the two agencies had been told by China’s housing bureau to stop making such figures publicly available for the time being.
Peng Peng, executive chairman of the Guangdong Society of Reform think tank, said the government’s recent actions showed that expectations management remained crucial for the property market.
“If expectations management fails, it could trigger broader risks such as widespread mortgage defaults and a surge in foreclosures,” he said.
In its post, Shanghai’s cyberspace regulator said it would continue to intensify efforts to clamp down on property market-related misinformation and update the public on the campaign’s progress.
According to Peng, recent transaction volume data indicated that the market was showing some signs of stabilising, although housing prices had yet to break their downward trend. The market also varied a lot by region, with a few projects still seeing strong demand, he added.
Shanghai’s secondary housing market has picked up slightly in recent weeks. The city recorded 22,943 sales of second-hand homes in November, a 24 per cent increase from October and the highest monthly total since May, according to data cited by the domestic media outlet Jiemian News.
However, some Chinese developers remain under intense pressure. Vanke, one of the country’s biggest property companies, had its credit rating downgraded last week after the firm proposed extending repayment for a 2 billion yuan (US$282 million) onshore bond.
Real estate investment also continues to decline. Investment was down 14.7 per cent year on year in the first 10 months of 2025, a slight acceleration from the 13.9 per cent fall recorded in the first nine months.
Lou Jiwei, a former Chinese finance minister, cautioned last month that the property slump could continue to act as a drag on China’s economic growth for another five years, as the market had still not bottomed out.
Authorities have tried to revive demand by gradually easing property purchase restrictions originally put in place to prevent speculative investment. Beijing, Shanghai and Shenzhen all relaxed buying limits between August and September, after Guangzhou became the first major city to fully scrap such curbs earlier this year.

data.title: Hong Kong Squash Open: Simmi Chan learns lesson as ex-champion Paul Coll survives fright
data.description:
Simmi Chan Sin-yuk said her squash career was just getting started after she waved goodbye to her American student life and settled back in Hong Kong.
The under-23 world championship runner-up in April, Chan exited the Milwaukee Hong Kong Squash Open on Wednesday after losing three tight games to world No 8 Tinne Gilis.
Chan said she was “happy I got close and left it all out there”, having not played at so high a level for a long time.
The 23-year-old world No 38 was pipped 11-9, 11-7 in the opening two games, before she was hauled back from 10-7 up to surrender the last 14-12.
“Having the game points made me more rushed, and I made a few mistakes,” Chan said. “I didn’t handle that the best way.
“I’ve always known I have to get better in those situations. The biggest lesson from the match was that I need to put consistent pressure on my opponent until I earn the point.”

Earlier this year, Chan completed her four-year economics and mathematics degree at New York’s Columbia University. She said relocating to Hong Kong Sports Institute (HKSI) was a “big change”.
“I was living the student life, there was pressure to maintain good academics and squash, but I was really happy,” she said.
“Being based at HKSI makes me far more focused on squash; it’s really good for my career.
“This is just the start for me. I can be fully committed to training … I’m looking forward to playing at a higher level.”
Gilis will be fighting for her family’s honour against Olivia Weaver in the last eight, after the American beat her sister, Nele Gilis, the former world No 4, 3-0 in their second-round encounter.
The 30-year-old Weaver endured an “emotional roller coaster” in Hong Kong last year, after injury forced her to withdraw from her semi-final.
“I want to win my first Platinum event and show everyone I can take these titles,” Weaver said. “That’s what I’m here to do.”

Satomi Watanabe of Japan and Egypt’s Amina Orfi also won to set up a quarter-final clash on Friday.
The 2023 men’s champion, Paul Coll, resisted a stirring fightback from Aly Abou Eleinen to win 3-2 to book a quarter-final meeting with England’s Marwan El Shorbagy.
World No 2 Coll, the husband of Nele Gilis, waltzed into a 2-0 lead before his Egyptian opponent claimed the next two games and led 7-3 in the decider. At that point, New Zealander Coll “switched back on” to win eight of the next nine points and prevail 11-8.
El Shorbagy progressed after beating Mohamed Abouelghar 3-1. Egyptian Abouelghar had overcome El Shorbagy on his way to winning last month’s China Open.
“I wanted revenge – you don’t want to lose twice to the same player,” Englishman El Shorbagy said. “I was thinking about the match so much.
“He’s playing well and had confidence, but I took that away from him during the match.”
On Thursday, the tournament will move from Hong Kong Squash Centre to a glass court at Ocean Park’s entry plaza.

data.title: Cuba faces blackout as electrical grid suffers partial collapse, Havana without power
data.description:
Cuba’s electrical grid suffered a partial collapse early on Wednesday, leaving the capital Havana and much of western Cuba in the dark, according to local media and eyewitness reports.
State-run media reporter Lazaro Manuel Alonso confirmed that four of the country’s westernmost provinces, from Pinar del Rio to Mayabeque, were without power.
It was not immediately clear what had caused the grid failure.
Havana’s skyline was largely dark before sunrise, with only hospitals and some tourist hotels still illuminated early on Wednesday.
Cuba’s oil-fired power plants, already obsolete and struggling to keep the lights on, experienced a full crisis last year as oil imports from Venezuela, Russia and Mexico dwindled.
Cuba’s grid has collapsed, partially or entirely, several times since.

Many across the Caribbean island nation are experiencing daily blackouts that extend 20 hours or more. Even Havana residents, once protected from the brunt of the outages, now regularly face 10 hours or more every day without electricity.
Cuba’s government blames fuel shortages, decrepit infrastructure and damage from Hurricane Melissa for worsening power outages.
US sanctions and a deep economic crisis have for years made it impossible for the government to buy enough fuel, forcing a growing dependence on allies and complicating Cuba’s efforts to keep the lights on.
But the island’s imports of crude and fuel in the first 10 months of 2025 fell more than a third compared with the same period in 2024 as key allies Mexico and Venezuela slashed supplies, according to shipping data and documents seen by Reuters.

data.title: Jane Wu lawsuit: US university seeks to dismiss case of China-born neuroscientist
data.description:
The legal battle over the death of Chinese-American neuroscientist Jane Wu officially began in a Chicago courtroom this week – a case that has shaken the research community and focused intense scrutiny on Northwestern University’s conduct.
A civil lawsuit filed by Wu’s family in June centres on how the university handled one of its most accomplished professors during the controversial “China Initiative”, alleging her suicide in July 2024 was a direct result of the school’s treatment amid the investigations.
Northwestern’s lawyers on Monday informed Cook County Judge Jonathan Green that the university had already moved to dismiss the case.
Relatives have accused Northwestern of marginalising the Feinberg School of Medicine molecular biologist – stripping her of funding, shutting down her lab, and ultimately having her forcibly removed from her office and taken to a psychiatric unit. They argued the actions were discriminatory and contributed to her death.

The school previously told NBC News that it “vehemently denies” the allegations. On Monday, Green asked the university to submit a more concise version of its motion within two weeks. A full hearing is scheduled for February.
Wu was the Dr Charles L. Mix Research Professor of Neurology and Psychiatry at Northwestern University before taking her own life at age 60. Born in Hefei, Anhui province, she went to the United States in the 1980s to pursue a PhD in cancer biology at Stanford University.
As a leading researcher on the genetic mechanisms behind brain disorders such as Parkinson’s and ALS, Wu received more than US$11 million in funding from the National Institutes of Health between 2006 and 2022, according to the NIH’s RePORT database.
However, within days of her death, Wu’s faculty profile page on the medical school website was taken down. Other web pages, including her publication and grant records on the Northwestern Scholar portal, also disappeared.
The response by the university was seen as unusual among Chinese-American researchers. At least two of them told the Post at the time that universities typically published an obituary and would maintain a late faculty member’s web pages for a period, neither of which happened in Wu’s case.
A previous Post investigation found that multiple former colleagues believed Wu was among the researchers targeted by NIH-backed investigations into undisclosed ties with China. These inquiries operated in parallel to the US Department of Justice’s now-defunct “China Initiative” investigations, launched during the first Trump administration to counter alleged economic espionage and technology transfer.


Both the NIH and Northwestern declined to comment at the time, but the family’s June complaint confirmed that investigations of Wu had begun in 2019.
According to the lawsuit, Wu was ultimately cleared by the NIH. However, Northwestern did not restore her standing. In February 2024, the university shut down part of her lab space, took away members of her research team with no explanation, and made it extremely difficult for her to obtain new funding and restart her work, the claim said.
Hundreds of scientists – most of them of Asian descent – were flagged by the NIH in the foreign interference investigations, often for failing to disclose overlapping funding or research ties in China. While only a few were criminally charged, many lost their jobs, suffered major financial losses or saw their careers destroyed.
If you have suicidal thoughts or know someone who is experiencing them, help is available. In Hong Kong, you can dial 18111 for the government-run Mental Health Support Hotline. You can also call +852 2896 0000 for The Samaritans or +852 2382 0000 for Suicide Prevention Services. In the US, call or text 988 or chat at 988lifeline.org for the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. For a list of other nations’ helplines, see this page.
